skill.name: bases
skill.description: Query Obsidian Bases through the local Bases Query plugin RPC endpoint and extract structured vault data.
skill.summary: Read and query Obsidian Bases through a local RPC plugin endpoint.
skill.author: ArtemXTech

security_audit.summary: Static command and network findings are mostly true positives in documentation examples that call a local Obsidian RPC service with curl and process results with jq. The weak cryptographic algorithm finding is a false positive from the RPC description, and no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Local RPC Commands Can Read Obsidian Vault Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill documents curl commands that query a local Obsidian Bases Query RPC endpoint. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can expose structured note metadata and frontmatter to the AI session when a user runs the commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly call a local RPC endpoint and return Obsidian base data. The intent is functional rather than malicious, but the data access risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Shell Pipeline Examples Require User Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill includes jq command examples for extracting fields from RPC responses. The examples are simple and fixed, but agents should not run shell commands against private vault data without user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are visible and limited to jq filters. Risk depends on user authorization and the sensitivity of local Obsidian data.

low_findings.title: Loopback Network Endpoint Is Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE FOR EXTERNAL NETWORK RISK: The hardcoded URL and IP address point to 127.0.0.1 for a local Obsidian plugin. No evidence found of outbound third-party exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every detected RPC URL uses 127.0.0.1, which is loopback. The only non-local URL is the installation link for the required plugin.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The line describes RPC usage and does not reference a weak hash, cipher, or cryptographic operation. No evidence found of cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata describing the Bases Query plugin RPC interface. There is no cryptographic algorithm or security primitive in the file.

low_findings.title: Plugin Installation Link Requires Source Trust
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE LOW RISK: The skill links to a GitHub repository for installation through BRAT. This is normal for Obsidian community plugins, but users should review the plugin before installing it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The GitHub URL is clearly an installation reference, not an automatic download or execution step. Supply-chain risk remains because it depends on an external plugin.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented curl POST Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs use of curl POST requests to a local RPC endpoint. This is intentional but should be treated as a permissioned data access operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and repeated. The endpoint is local, which reduces but does not remove privacy risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Localhost RPC Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill hardcodes a localhost RPC endpoint at 127.0.0.1:27125. This limits network scope to the local machine but can access local vault data through the plugin.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded loopback endpoint is clearly present. The security impact depends on what the installed Obsidian plugin exposes.

content.user_title: Query Obsidian Bases from AI Tools
content.value_statement: Obsidian Bases data can be hard to inspect from an AI coding session. This skill shows Claude, Codex, and Claude Code how to query a local Bases RPC plugin and summarize structured note data.
actual_capabilities: Lists available Obsidian bases through the local RPC endpoint.
Queries a named base and view when the user provides the path and view name.
Retrieves schema information to discover available fields.
Shows expected response fields such as path, name, and frontmatter.
Demonstrates jq filters for extracting names and selected frontmatter values.
limitations: Requires the Obsidian Bases Query plugin to be installed and running.
Only documents access through the localhost RPC endpoint.
Does not provide authentication, authorization, or plugin setup automation.
Can only query data exposed by the installed plugin and selected base view.

use_cases.title: Review project note status
use_cases.description: Query a project base and summarize active, blocked, or completed notes.

use_cases.title: Inspect vault schemas
use_cases.description: Discover available fields before building filters, dashboards, or reports.

use_cases.title: Extract structured note lists
use_cases.description: Pull names and selected frontmatter values from a base view for quick review.

prompt_templates.title: List my bases
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bases skill to list available Obsidian bases from the local RPC endpoint. Ask before running any command.

prompt_templates.title: Query a specific view
prompt_templates.prompt: Query the Obsidian base at [base path] and view [view name]. Summarize the returned records by name and status.

prompt_templates.title: Discover fields first
prompt_templates.prompt: Get the schema for [base path] and [view name], then recommend useful fields for a concise report.

prompt_templates.title: Create a focused summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Query [base path] view [view name], extract names and selected frontmatter fields, and summarize trends without exposing unrelated notes.

output_examples.output: A short list of base names found through the local plugin, with a note that results came from the local vault.

output_examples.input: Query my Projects base and summarize active items.
output_examples.output: A grouped summary of active project notes, including note names and selected status fields from the requested view.

output_examples.input: Show the schema for my Reading view.
output_examples.output: A readable list of available fields, followed by suggestions for useful fields to include in a report.

best_practices: Ask for user approval before running commands that read local vault data.
Query the schema before assuming field names in a base view.
Limit summaries to the base, view, and fields requested by the user.
anti_patterns: Do not run RPC queries against private notes without explicit user consent.
Do not assume the plugin is installed or reachable before checking.
Do not expose full frontmatter when the user only requested a narrow summary.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to the local Obsidian Bases Query plugin RPC endpoint on the user's machine.

faq.question: Does it send data to an external service?
faq.answer: The documented RPC endpoint is localhost. AI tools may still include returned data in the active session.

faq.question: What must be installed first?
faq.answer: The Obsidian Bases Query plugin must be installed and running in Obsidian.

faq.question: Can it query any Obsidian note?
faq.answer: It queries data exposed by the plugin for the selected base and view.

faq.question: Why does it use curl and jq?
faq.answer: curl sends local RPC requests, and jq filters the returned structured data.

faq.question: Is this safe for private vaults?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Approve each query and request only the fields needed for the task.
